verses.text: The psalmist sings of God's mercies for ever, recalling His covenant with David: 'thy seed will I establish for ever.' He rests on God's sworn promise. The covenant with David points to the everlasting throne of Christ.

The heavens praise God's wonders, and none in heaven can be compared to Him, feared above all the holy ones. He is incomparable. The God who keeps His covenant is mightier than all powers above.
The heavens praise God's wonders, and none in heaven can be compared to Him, feared above all the holy ones. He is incomparable. The God who keeps His covenant is mightier than all powers above.

The heavens praise God's wonders, and none in heaven can be compared to Him, feared above all the holy ones. He is incomparable. The God who keeps His covenant is mightier than all powers above.
He rules the raging sea, scattered His foes, and owns the heavens and earth, His arm strong and His right hand high. All creation is His. The God of such power is well able to keep His word.
He rules the raging sea, scattered His foes, and owns the heavens and earth, His arm strong and His right hand high. All creation is His. The God of such power is well able to keep His word.

'Justice and judgment are the habitation of thy throne'; blessed are the people who know the joyful sound and walk in His light. His rule is righteous. To know and rejoice in God is the people's happiness and strength.

God recalls choosing David, anointing him with holy oil, promising to uphold and strengthen him against every foe. The king is God's gift and care. What God establishes, He defends.

God's faithfulness and mercy shall be with him, his power exalted, and he shall cry, 'Thou art my father, my God, and the rock of my salvation.' Sonship marks the king. This intimacy is fulfilled in Christ, the true Son.

God's faithfulness and mercy shall be with him, his power exalted, and he shall cry, 'Thou art my father, my God, and the rock of my salvation.' Sonship marks the king. This intimacy is fulfilled in Christ, the true Son.

God's faithfulness and mercy shall be with him, his power exalted, and he shall cry, 'Thou art my father, my God, and the rock of my salvation.' Sonship marks the king. This intimacy is fulfilled in Christ, the true Son.

God will make him 'my firstborn, higher than the kings of the earth,' his throne enduring as the days of heaven. Pre-eminence is promised. The Davidic king foreshadows the exalted, everlasting reign of Christ.

God will make him 'my firstborn, higher than the kings of the earth,' his throne enduring as the days of heaven. Pre-eminence is promised. The Davidic king foreshadows the exalted, everlasting reign of Christ.

God will make him 'my firstborn, higher than the kings of the earth,' his throne enduring as the days of heaven. Pre-eminence is promised. The Davidic king foreshadows the exalted, everlasting reign of Christ.

If David's sons forsake God's law, He will chastise them with the rod, 'nevertheless my lovingkindness will I not utterly take.' Discipline does not cancel covenant. God corrects His own but never abandons His promise.

If David's sons forsake God's law, He will chastise them with the rod, 'nevertheless my lovingkindness will I not utterly take.' Discipline does not cancel covenant. God corrects His own but never abandons His promise.
God has sworn by His holiness that David's seed and throne shall endure for ever, 'as the sun' and 'as the moon.' His oath is unbreakable. The certainty of the covenant rests on God's own holy word.
God has sworn by His holiness that David's seed and throne shall endure for ever, 'as the sun' and 'as the moon.' His oath is unbreakable. The certainty of the covenant rests on God's own holy word.

Yet now, the psalmist laments, God seems to have cast off and abhorred His anointed, breaking down his defences. Promise and present ruin clash. Faith struggles when God's word and our circumstances seem opposed.

The king's enemies rejoice, his glory is cast down, and his days are shortened with shame. The fall seems complete. The believer may see God's cause humbled and not understand why.

'How long, LORD? wilt thou hide thyself for ever?' he cries, owning that every man must see death. He pleads from human frailty. Our short, frail lives make us long the more for God to act.

'Lord, where are thy former lovingkindnesses?' he asks, begging God to remember His servants' reproach; yet he ends, 'Blessed be the LORD for evermore.' Lament closes in praise. Even unanswered, faith blesses the God whose covenant cannot fail.
